paper.title_en: Impact of 3D macro physics and nuclear physics on the s-process during He-flashes in Asymptotic Giant Branch stars
paper.abstract_en: The $\mathrm { He}$-intershell in Asymptotic Giant Branch stars is a main site for the s-process, but 1D stellar models rely on the mixing length theory, which does not match the radial velocity profiles of 3D hydrodynamic simulations of $\mathrm { He}$-flashes. We investigate how 3D macro physics-informed mixing impacts the nucleosynthesis of s-nuclei. We post-process the pulse-driven convective zone of the $M_\textrm{ZAMS}=3\;\mathrm {\textrm{M}_\odot }$, $Z=0.01$ model from the NuGrid stellar data set. Applying a downturn to velocities at the boundary and increasing velocities across the shell as obtained in previous results, we find that, within a single $\mathrm { He}$-flash, adopting 3D-inspired mixing profiles leads to only minor variations in the production of $s$-process isotopes, typically at the level of fractions of a $\delta $ point in zirconium and barium isotopic ratios. In contrast, nuclear physics uncertainties, particularly those associated with the $\mathrm {^{22}Ne}(\alpha ,n)\mathrm {^{25}Mg}$ neutron source, produce significantly larger effects. Our results nevertheless suggest that the decreased mixing efficiency at the bottom of the pulse-driven convection zone predicted by hydrodynamic simulations may lead to structural changes if implemented self-consistently in full stellar evolution models from the onset of the AGB phase, potentially affecting the efficiency of convection-induced mixing beneath the 1D Schwarzschild boundary of the $\mathrm { He}$ intershell thermal pulses boundary. However, establishing robust parametrisations of such effects will require 3D hydrodynamic simulations of the $\mathrm { He}$-flash that include sufficiently deep stable layers below the convective region.
